title: 1.1: Reports memory usage patterns using line-by-line tracemalloc output showing allocations in Paperless source files (e.g., shows specific files like `paperless_tesseract/parsers.py` with allocation sizes).
1.2: States the memory impact difference in using Django .all() with caching and .iterator() with streaming (e.g., .all() loads all records into memory while .iterator() streams one at a time).

1.3: Reports that memory accumulates across document batches but remains stable for single files (e.g., batch processing shows incremental memory growth while single files show peak-then-release pattern).
1.4: States which pattern or condition triggers the spike in memory (e.g. large metadata size).

1.5: Identifies that Python memory arenas retain memory for reuse rather than immediately releasing it to the OS (e.g., arenas keep allocated blocks for future allocations even after objects are garbage collected).
1.6: Maps reference chains showing how document objects retain metadata in memory (e.g., shows Document→Metadata→Tag→Classifier chain).
1.7: States that different document types show varying memory patterns (e.g., PDFs consume more memory than text files, images show different allocation patterns).

1.8: Reports that tracemalloc identifies pikepdf.models.metadata as a major memory allocation source during PDF metadata extraction (e.g., shows allocations like pikepdf/models/metadata.py with multi-MB sizes).
2.1: Claims to use valgrind or other C-level profiling tools on Python code.
